## Why k-nearest neighbors belongs early in the course

`k`-nearest neighbors gives students one of the clearest pictures of supervised
learning: keep the training data, measure similarity, and let nearby examples vote.
There is almost no hidden machinery.

That simplicity makes kNN useful in `TEM 0052`. It is easy to explain, easy to test,
and sensitive to workflow choices in exactly the way we want students to notice.

## The prediction rule

For a new point `x`, the algorithm finds the `k` closest training observations under
a distance rule such as Euclidean distance and uses those neighbors to make the
prediction.

In classification, the usual rule is majority vote among the neighbors. In
regression, the same basic logic averages nearby outcomes instead.

The model is therefore nonparametric in a practical sense: it does not summarize the
training data with one compact equation the way linear or logistic regression does.

## What really matters in practice

kNN looks simple, but the performance depends heavily on a few design choices:

- the value of `k`,
- the feature scaling rule,
- the distance metric,
- and whether irrelevant features are allowed to dominate the geometry.

That is why a casual kNN fit can be misleading. If one variable is measured on a much
larger scale than the others, the neighborhood calculation can be distorted before the
model has even started to classify anything.

## kNN is a bias-variance lesson in one model

Small `k` usually means a very flexible model. It can track local detail, but it can
also react strongly to noise. Large `k` smooths the decision rule and reduces
variance, but it can blur real class boundaries.

That trade-off is exactly why kNN is a good teaching model for:

- bias versus variance,
- cross-validation,
- and the difference between a nice training fit and a reliable out-of-sample result.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students why `k = 1` is often attractive on the training set and why that is
usually the wrong reason to keep it.
:::

## Where kNN fits in `TEM 0052`

kNN is one of the first classification baselines in the course. It helps students see
that prediction quality can depend as much on preprocessing and tuning as on the
choice between algorithm families.

It also pairs naturally with the model-assessment lab, where students compare kNN,
logistic regression, and tree-based alternatives under the same resampling rule.

## What to report

When students present a kNN workflow in `TEM 0052`, they should usually report:

- how features were scaled,
- which grid of `k` values was considered,
- which resampling rule selected the final `k`,
- how the chosen model compared with logistic regression or other baselines,
- and one short explanation of whether the final choice looks robust or overly
  sensitive to preprocessing and noise.
